topic: Ancient South India — Sangam Age, Pallavas & Chalukyas

questions.explanation: The Tirukkural (1,330 couplets on ethics, polity, and love) was written by Tiruvalluvar. It is called the 'Tamil Veda' and has been translated into 60+ languages. Ilango Adigal wrote Silappatikaram; Tolkappiyar wrote Tolkappiyam (Tamil grammar); Sattanar wrote Manimekalai.

questions.text: The 'Aihole Inscription' records the victory of Chalukya king Pulakesin II over which king?
questions.explanation: The Aihole Inscription (composed by poet Ravikirti) records Pulakesin II's victory over Harsha at the Narmada River, stopping Harsha's southern expansion. This inscription also helps date Pulakesin II's reign and provides valuable historical data. Pulakesin II stopped Harsha at the Narmada — the boundary remained.

questions.text: The Pancha Rathas (Five Rathas) at Mahabalipuram were built by the:
questions.explanation: The Pancha Rathas (five monolithic rock-cut chariot-temples) at Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram) were built by the Pallavas during the reign of Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla) — named 'Mamallapuram' after him. They represent five different architectural styles. Named after the five Pandavas and Draupadi.

questions.text: Which of the following is the oldest surviving Tamil text?
questions.explanation: Tolkappiyam by Tolkappiyar is the oldest surviving Tamil text — a grammar and poetics manual. While the Sangam anthologies were compiled around the same time, Tolkappiyam is considered the oldest complete surviving work. The Tirukkural and epics came somewhat later within the Sangam period.

questions.text: What are the emblems of the three Tamil Sangam kingdoms in the correct order (Chera, Chola, Pandya)?
questions.explanation: Chera = Bow; Chola = Tiger; Pandya = Fish. A useful mnemonic: alphabetical order of kingdom names (Chera, Chola, Pandya) matches the sequence Bow → Tiger → Fish. These emblems appear on royal seals, flags, and coins and are directly tested in SSC.

questions.text: The Kailasanatha Temple at Kanchipuram (the first full structural stone temple in South India) was built by which Pallava ruler?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha Temple at Kanchipuram was built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha, 700–728 CE). It is the oldest structural stone temple in Kanchipuram (as opposed to rock-cut). Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla) built the rock-cut monuments at Mahabalipuram. These two rulers are frequently confused.

questions.text: Which Satavahana ruler is described as 'Destroyer of Sakas, Yavanas and Pahlavas' in the Nasik Inscription?
questions.explanation: Gautamiputra Satakarni (106–130 CE) is described in the Nasik Praśasti (composed by his mother Gautami Balashri) as 'destroyer of Sakas (Scythians), Yavanas (Greeks), and Pahlavas (Parthians)' — indicating he defeated these three foreign ruling groups. This is the primary source for his achievements.

questions.text: The Arjuna's Penance rock relief at Mahabalipuram is the largest bas-relief in the world. Its alternative name is:
options: Vishnu's Sleep on the Cosmic Ocean
questions.explanation: Arjuna's Penance at Mahabalipuram is also called 'Descent of Ganga' (Gangavataran) — the two-panel bas-relief (~27 × 9 metres) carved from a single granite outcrop. It depicts the mythological descent of the River Ganga from heaven, with hundreds of figures including serpents (nagas), gods, animals, and humans.

questions.text: The Roman trading post at Arikamedu (near Pondicherry) is significant because it proves:
options: Direct commercial contact between Rome and Sangam-era Tamil kingdoms
Tamil merchants ruled Rome in the 1st century CE
questions.explanation: Arikamedu (ancient Poduke) on the Coromandel coast shows clear Roman presence — Mediterranean amphorae, glassware, lamps, and pottery have been found there. This confirms direct Roman trade with Tamil Nadu described in Sangam literature and Roman texts (Pliny the Elder's Natural History mentions trade with 'Poduke').

questions.text: Silappatikaram, the Tamil epic, is the story of:
options: Kannagi who burns Madurai after her husband Kovalan is unjustly executed
The war between the Cheras and the Pandyas
questions.explanation: Silappatikaram ('The Tale of an Anklet') by Ilango Adigal tells the story of Kovalan and Kannagi. Kovalan left his faithful wife Kannagi for a dancer (Madhavi), lost his fortune, and tried to sell Kannagi's anklet in Madurai — but was falsely accused of theft and executed by the Pandya king's order. Kannagi proved his innocence and, in her grief, burned Madurai.

questions.text: The Dravidian temple style is characterised by a pyramidal tower called:
questions.explanation: In Dravidian (South Indian) temple architecture, the tower over the sanctum sanctorum (garbhagriha) is called Vimana — and it is pyramidal in shape (tiered). The Gopuram (towering gateway) is the entrance tower — often taller than the Vimana in later temples. In North Indian (Nagara) style, the tower is called Shikhara and is curvilinear.

questions.text: Who built the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple (8th century CE) was built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) — the same ruler who built the Kailasanatha Temple at Kanchipuram. It is one of the oldest surviving structural temples in South India and is UNESCO World Heritage Site. It faces east so the rising sun illuminates the sanctum.

questions.text: The Chalukya king Pulakesin II was defeated and killed by which Pallava ruler?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla) defeated Chalukya Pulakesin II in battle (~642 CE) — Pulakesin II died in the conflict or its aftermath. Mamalla earned the title 'Vatapikonda' (Conqueror of Vatapi/Badami — the Chalukya capital). It is the reverse of Pulakesin II's earlier defeat of Harsha.

questions.text: Which port was the most important for Roman trade with the Chola kingdom?
questions.explanation: Puhar (Kaveripattinam) was the Chola's main port city on the Coromandel Coast — described in the Sangam text Pattinappalai as a cosmopolitan trading hub. Muzaris (Kodungallur, Kerala) was the main port for the Chera kingdom. Both are significant for Roman trade.

questions.text: Manimekalai, the sequel to Silappatikaram, was written by:
questions.explanation: Manimekalai was written by Sattanar (Merchant Sattanar) and is the sequel to Silappatikaram. It follows Manimekalai — the daughter of Kovalan and the dancer Madhavi — on her spiritual journey toward Buddhism. It is a significant Buddhist literary work in Tamil.

questions.text: The Amarvati Stupa (Andhra Pradesh) was a major Buddhist monument built under the patronage of:
questions.explanation: The Amaravati Stupa (on the Krishna River, Andhra Pradesh) was expanded and decorated primarily under Satavahana patronage (1st century BCE–3rd century CE). Its white marble sculptural panels are considered the finest example of Andhra school Buddhist art. The sculptures are now in Chennai Museum and the British Museum.
